sentences.text: Yeah, with my brother, my sister.
Nojech and Chuma.
When the doors opened from the train, there were SS.
They took us on the appel field.
And there they start what we brought, what we take in.
They start to examine things.
And later on, we were put in in the barracks, like they used to call this like for horses or whatever it was.
And this was our destiny.
There we were.
And I remember my brother said, my gosh, what are we going to do?
I said to him, Nojech, this is it.
Forget about what was yesterday.
This, you have to go, and that's all.
And then it was an appel the first day on the field.
And the head of the SS, his name was Nell, and they start to tell us what to do and how to behave.
And the next day-- I don't know for what and why-- they took one man what had come with the same transport with us.
His name was Masler.
And they took him out in the little forest there, and they shot him.
That was the beginning.
I have no idea whatsoever.
No, no uniform at all.
That's what we did wear.
Then whatever they want, they took it away from us.
And they registered us.
Not the-- the Germans-- there were right away Jewish people, what they did, mostly from Radom.
They did come in the morning.
We did come in the afternoon.
And they did a register.
It was very, very simple registration.
And then whatever we had to go to work.
There were-- a lot of people were-- there-- outside work.
The steinbruch they used to call.
And that was very, very hard.
They'd make cement something, like cement.
That's what it was.
That call is in German.
I don't know how to translate it.
I worked in the shop, the uniforms, for they send it away for the Germans.
Yeah.
We had to go to work.
And later on, like in the morning, we got some s-- coffee.
Don't ask.
And then we got-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at home-- when we went home, we got a soup.
The soup was more water than soup, but this is the way that was, and we couldn't help it.
There were some people.
Well, they had money.
And outside the camp from Blizyn, through the fence, some Polish people, they threw it over, some bread.
You threw some money.
And they were on the-- the Ukrainians, they, they, they-- they'd looked after this.
And probably, they give some money to the Ukraines, and they didn't do anything so.
Everybody tried to help himself, whatever was possible.
Yeah I got sick once.
I got a typhus.
In Blizyn.
And somehow I survive.
A lot of people, my friends, they didn't survive from typhus-- a lot of people.
No.
There was-- it's called a hospital, and I was there, in the hospital.
And to be honest, I didn't know even-- I knew typhus, but I know the only thing-- I, I was sick, very sick.
And somehow, someway, I survived.
Portion.
Yes.
We lined up to receive the por-- like soup.
Bread-- sometime they give like 10 people for a bread.
And everything, you know, whoever dis-- displayed the bread-- that was just terrible.
It was measured so by, by, by in-- by inch.
Inch is nothing.
That everybody got the same potion.
God forbid somebody had a little bit bigger or whatever it is was murder.
Did-- everybody did try not to be like this.
They had-- some of them, they made a little scale, you know, from a plate and weighed.
Mostly.
Mostly.
It was not a responsibility.
Whoever ta-- we thought he can do it, he did it.
No, never.
Sometime I think-- if I remember correctly-- if we got the ready portion right from the kitchen, too.
I don't remember this.
In Blizyn, yes.
In Blizyn, yes, because it was not divided.
So there were some couples there.
They were-- they were married, husband and wife, but they, they were not together.
They were in a separate barracks.
Like I did see often every day my sister.
I could have come over there.
As a matter of fact, we played soccer in Blizyn.
I remember all day, La-- Radom, Piatkow, Starachowice, everybody did play against our team, Tomaszow.
And we won.
We obvious won.
We were the best.
So the Jewish-- you know, the Jewish-- what-- he was the head in Blizyn.
He did play soccer too.
His name was Minzberg.
He was a very good guy, very good guy.
And we won, we got a extra soup.
And you know, the Germans, they did watch us play.
Till we when out to this, to Birkenau.
I just-- I just marked it like today.
I don't--
Yeah.
The only thing I know, we were there till June 1944.
And then we went to Oranienburg Sachsenhausen for a short period of time.
Also in the-- in the-- no, in the train.
The cattle train, yeah.
Not different at all.
[INAUDIBLE] I think it was about seven, eight hours, or more.
I don't remember correctly.
Or more, even.
At the night.
Oh, did we have it that.
That welcome, I will never forget in my life.
The yelling, the screaming, the, the, the, the-- what the SS did with the dogs that I, I really don't know how everybody come out alive.
Not-- I wouldn't say everybody.
But the people from Blizyn, when we did come to Birkenau, I don't know or we had our good name from the Germans from Blizyn that we didn't-- we didn't go in a selection.
We went right-- in Birkenau, we went into the shower there, but we didn't go in a selection.
Welcome
The Germans.
Raus!
Raus!
Raus!
They made it such that everybody got so scared.
Raus, that means, faster, faster, get out, get out.
Yeah?
We assembled and march towards the shower.
And we didn't know the shower was there in that time right near the crematorium.
We didn't know anything about.
Maybe some people knew, but I personally didn't know anything.
In that time in Birkenau, we get the uniforms--
--the, the, the stripe uniforms.
Everybody got.
We didn't-- didn't know anything about crematoriums or chimneys where-- we went to the, like I said, to the shower.
Where there is shower, we saw the chimney.
And if we did ask somebody what is this, over there what it is-- for they'd been before-- they said the-- some, they made a joke.
They said, oh, oh, you will go with the-- with the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through the chimney, something like this.
The only thing we saw that was was in night, that was the sky-- red, burn all day and night.
The registration was this.
The next day, they put the numbers on the hand.
And we did-- I did stay with my brother in the same-- next to each other.
And we put in the arm like this.
And somebody behind, they put the number on.
Before people did come, they just put it down here.
I have a number right here.
You see--
If you stay like this, comes out even.
And if you stay like this, comes out the opposite way.
Right.
The only thing they forgot here to put it then, a B.
Because I didn't know.
I saw I haven't got a B.
My brother's got a B.
Yeah, yeah.
It looks like it was overlooked.
I didn't know.
Yeah.
Yeah.
They made it like-- they called us [GERMAN].
You know what that means?
That means the lous-- louse on the strip because it was cut right in the middle, like this.
By, by and inch, an inch and a half, a stripe.
Yeah.
There were a lot of colors.
Some, they had red.
Some, they had black.
And I forgot what we had.
I think J, Jude.
Yeah, a yellow-- ma-- Jude.
That's right.
I think was a, a tri-- a triangle there.
I don't remember this.
No.
I didn't work at all.
I don't know.
The only thing there was the head from the barrack, his name was Pinkus.
He was from Paris-- a Jewish guy, a very bad guy.
For somehow, some reason, he picked me, and he said, you're going to cook for me.
And I remember he said, make me some carrots.
Cook me some carrots.
I put in carrots and put in water.
And he came over to me.
He said, you said you are a cook.
Is this the way to cook carrots?
And I looked at him, and he did see that I'm green.
And he said, I'm going to knock your teeth out right away.
Is this the way that you cook carrot?
I said, how?
Carrots, you cook on sugar.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I said, OK.
Give me sugar.
He said, that's fair.
I took yours.
I cooked it.
I have to give you sugar.
I said, from where can I get it?
So he sent me into the kitchen because they, they had a little bit more.
They could get whatever they want.
And go into Sasha and say that I sent you.
And you got me sugar.
And then, then I cooked the carrots.
Where did you cook them, in the barrack?
In the barrack.
They had a little oven and coals and wood.
I remember, I cooked the carrots, and then they were ready.
I threw it ov-- over the wire, the electric wire, to my sister.
And she couldn't-- till now, she doesn't know how I did it.
For him?
Oh, yeah.
The mostly, a little bit for her.
Correct.
I think he had a trial after the war.
They catched him.
Yeah.
Pinkus was his name.
Oh, in the barracks, you know, some people were sick.
And you had to stay on appell, and they couldn't stay.
They fall, and they-- oye, oye, oye.
It was terrible, just terrible, in one word.
They were like-- they, they're counting, counting the, the people or nobody did escape or whatever it is.
They counted.
In-- well, the same thing as it was in Blizyn.
They just give it soup.
They broughted soup over to every barrack.
And people from the barrack, they divide it to everybody.
Soup-- I want to had a little bit better, a little bit, you know, [INAUDIBLE] soup, a little bit [INAUDIBLE] soup.
Later on.
Yeah.
Because later on-- right the second day, you knew when we arrived that this is crematoriums, and people are getting gassed there.
And like when we were there already, people later on did come from Lodz.
And some people, they right away went into the gas and the children.
So they children, a lot of children.
They came from Wilno.
I remember a lot of-- a lot of children.
To see them?
Yeah, you could see them from one barrack to the other.
You can see them.
You could walk there too.
Because it was lengthwise, and the barracks was-- they were standing like this, like this, like this.
And you could go there.
Yeah, yeah.
Oh yeah.
Sure, that was-- it was open.
I mean that everybody could see it from far away.
No.
No.
No.
The only thing, I, I know like, like we went into the shower, yeah?
That was a real shower.
And they-- what I've been told in the-- in the crematoriums-- maybe it was even the same what I went in, the shower, that they threw it in the gas through the roof.
This, I don't know.
But we were people who did arrive from Blizyn.
They never got gassed, none of us.
Maybe later on, somebody got sick, and they took us to the gas chamber.
No.
Till November.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was in Birkenau.
And from Birkenau, we went for a very short period of time to Oranienburg and Sachsenhausen, maybe two weeks altogether.
To Sachsenhausen?
To Oranienburg.
We were there I think a few days, a week.
And in Sachsenhausen also so the same time.
And from Sachsenhausen, we were transport already to Ohrdruf in very good train, in a train, a passenger train.
By, by also the cattle, the cattle one.
There wasn't-- we did walk from Oranienburg to Sachsenhausen.
It wasn't very far.
Nothing.
We did sleep in a-- in a very, very big hole where they made the airplanes.
That's all.
That's what I did see.
We didn't see any other people too. in one camp, we saw one Pollack there, I remember.
They were a little bit farther outside.
And the-- and same Oranienburg, a little bit farther.
We didn't see it.
Yeah.
By foot.
Walked.
Not long.
I would say about three hours.
We pressed by a little city.
We did see German people, and they did see us walking on the street.
No.
No.
The same thing, Ukrainians, some Germans too in that time.
No, not as far as I know.
I have no idea.
I have no idea.
Oh, would be about-- for sure about 1,000 or between 1,000 and 1,500, for sure.
With mine brother all the time.
Yeah.
All the time, and some friend too.
Not me.
He had some discussion I know with some other people.
But I said to him, what are you going to do?
He, he wouldn't be able to do it, not him.
OK.
